text: # Geopolitics under the microscope: impact on retail and leisure

metadata.source_title: Geopolitics under the microscope: impact on retail and leisure

text: [Analysis](https://www.abnamro.nl/nl/zakelijk/insights/sectoren-en-trends/analyses/index.html)

metadata.source_title: Geopolitics under the microscope: impact on retail and leisure

text: [Retail](https://www.abnamro.nl/nl/zakelijk/insights/sectoren-en-trends/retail/index.html)

metadata.source_title: Geopolitics under the microscope: impact on retail and leisure

text: [Gerarda Westerhuis](https://www.abnamro.nl/nl/zakelijk/insights/authors/gerarda-westerhuis/index.html)

metadata.source_title: Geopolitics under the microscope: impact on retail and leisure

text: The direct impact of import duties between the US and Europe is limited, but entrepreneurs in retail and leisure can be affected indirectly. US tariffs on Chinese products increase the risk of dumping on the European market. A falling dollar affects export and import of products and travel between the US and the Netherlands. Economic growth is declining due to cooling of world trade. Consumers may be postponing major expenses.

metadata.source_title: Geopolitics under the microscope: impact on retail and leisure

text: Despite the 90-day pause, the 10 percent universal tariff remains in effect on all goods imported into the U.S., as do separate 25 percent tariffs on steel and aluminum and cars and auto parts. The direct effect on Dutch retailers of these American import duties is limited. Consumer goods such as clothing and furniture make up only a small part of the total exports from the Netherlands to the US, which amounted to more than 38 billion euros in 2024. Retail entrepreneurs who export these products to the US may notice import duties of 10 percent.

metadata.source_title: Geopolitics under the microscope: impact on retail and leisure

text: Vice versa, the EU has also imposed a 90-day pause on counter-tariffs on US products. If these European import duties do come into effect, they will have limited influence on Dutch retailers. Although we do not know exactly how much retail products the Netherlands imports from the US, data from the Central Bureau of Statistics (CBS) clearly shows that the majority of the almost 60 billion euros in imported goods in 2024 will consist of other products, such as crude oil, natural gas and medical instruments and devices. The share of retail products is therefore very small.

metadata.source_title: Geopolitics under the microscope: impact on retail and leisure

text: ## Fear of dumping Chinese products on the European market

metadata.source_title: Geopolitics under the microscope: impact on retail and leisure

text: Due to the very high US import duties on Chinese products of 145 percent, European policymakers and entrepreneurs fear that very low-priced Chinese products will flood the European market. Dumping, as it is called, can disrupt market dynamics. Consumer electronics are (temporarily) exempt from import duties. This reduces the risk of dumping of these products on the European market. The exemption is intended to spare American companies such as Apple, which have much of their production in China. This exemption also limits price increases for American consumers.

metadata.source_title: Geopolitics under the microscope: impact on retail and leisure

text: Recent Chinese export figures do not yet indicate dumping on the European market, but this could change quickly, the [Economic Bureau of ABN AMRO](https://www.abnamro.com/research/nl/onze-research/wereldhandel-frontloading-gaat-vooraf-aan- Rateschok) says. Retailers and wholesalers who import items such as toys or clothing from China may benefit from dumping; After all, they can purchase their products at lower prices. Dutch retailers import relatively a lot from China. Electronics in particular, such as modems and computers, are purchased relatively frequently: together accounting for almost 21 percent of total imports. But clothing (5.5 percent) and furniture (2.5 percent) also often come from China. These products are popular because of their low prices.

metadata.source_title: Geopolitics under the microscope: impact on retail and leisure

text: At the same time, price dumping can be disadvantageous for retailers and wholesalers who also produce consumer products themselves (vertical integration). Examples include Rituals or SuitSupply, which manufacture their own products and sell them through their own stores or online platforms. Or HEMA, which offers a wide range of own brand products through a traditional store formula. As producers, such companies may have difficulty continuing to compete with low-priced Chinese products, as is the case for all 'pure' production companies. We previously saw this with electric cars (EVs), where a Chinese export wave led to significant price pressure in Europe. That was beneficial for consumers, but undermined for European car manufacturers. EU policymakers are aware of these risks, are preparing possible measures and are in discussions with Beijing about this.

metadata.source_title: Geopolitics under the microscope: impact on retail and leisure

text: The question is to what extent Dutch retailers and wholesalers can actually benefit from cheaper products from China. Dutch retailers have been struggling with direct competition from China for some time. For example, Chinese platforms such as Shein and Temu flood the European market with cheap products, sometimes even below cost price to gain market share. They bypass Dutch retailers and wholesalers and sell directly to consumers. The Thuiswinkel Market Monitor shows that of all online cross-border purchases, Dutch consumers make most purchases at Chinese web shops. The number of online purchases at Chinese web shops has increased by 37 percent in 2024 compared to 2023. The European Commission has long been calling for measures to level the playing field, for example by levying taxes on all small packages under 150 euros. [China](https://www.abnamro.com/research/nl/onze-research/wereldhandel-frontloading-gaat-vooraf-aan- Rateschok) currently needs to properly manage its relations with important trading partners, such as the EU. This potentially gives Brussels a strong negotiating position vis-à-vis Beijing to obtain favorable trade and investment conditions and prevent Chinese dumping on the European market.

metadata.source_title: Geopolitics under the microscope: impact on retail and leisure

text: As long as China continues to produce on a large scale and at low costs and gains market share in retail, retailers in the Netherlands must remain alert. What is certain is that prices in Europe remain unpredictable and the market is not only dependent on geopolitical developments, but also on Chinese export strategies.

metadata.source_title: Geopolitics under the microscope: impact on retail and leisure

text: ## Impact of the dollar exchange rate on export and import of products

metadata.source_title: Geopolitics under the microscope: impact on retail and leisure

text: The dollar rate has fallen in recent months from 98 euro cents on January 12, 2025 to 88 euro cents on May 1. This makes exports from the Netherlands to the US more expensive and imports cheaper. As we saw, Dutch retailers import and export relatively little to and from the US. But Dutch brands that export clothing and furniture to the US may notice this lower dollar rate on top of the universal tax rate of 10 percent. Due to the lower dollar exchange rate, they therefore have to compete 'double' with the relatively cheap products from American soil. The extent to which depends on the price elasticity: is there a substitute available or is it a more luxurious or unique product?

metadata.source_title: Geopolitics under the microscope: impact on retail and leisure

text: The lower dollar exchange rate may have more impact for retailers who import a relatively large amount from China. If this is paid in dollars, purchasing becomes cheaper for retailers. Sea transport in containers will also become cheaper with a lower dollar exchange rate; this almost always takes place in dollars. However, it does not mean that consumers will immediately notice these lower purchasing and transport costs. These often involve long-term contracts and the price can only be negotiated with a new contract.

metadata.source_title: Geopolitics under the microscope: impact on retail and leisure

text: Although import duties on goods do not have a direct impact on the travel industry, the industry does notice the indirect effects. Due to all the uncertainty surrounding the American import duties - there is already talk of a decline in economic growth worldwide - market researcher Skift has revised downwards its growth expectations for the global travel industry. At the beginning of the year, growth of 6 to 9 percent was expected, but this has now been reduced to a modest growth of 2 to 5 percent.

metadata.source_title: Geopolitics under the microscope: impact on retail and leisure

text: In the Netherlands we already see signs that fewer Americans are visiting the country. The expected increase in inflation could seriously affect the purchasing power of American households. A weaker dollar also makes vacations more expensive for American tourists in Europe. The Netherlands is therefore becoming less attractive as a destination. Fewer dollars per euro means less purchasing power and therefore lower spending in catering, museums and shops.

metadata.source_title: Geopolitics under the microscope: impact on retail and leisure

text: For the Dutch leisure economy, this means that fewer Americans will visit our country this year, as well as a decrease in spending per American visitor. Before Trump became president, the Netherlands Bureau for Tourism & Conventions (NBTC) expected a 5 percent increase in the number of tourists from the US to almost 1.6 million overnight guests. This concerns approximately 7 percent of all incoming tourism.

metadata.source_title: Geopolitics under the microscope: impact on retail and leisure

text: Lower growth in tourism from the US affects hotels, especially Amsterdam ones that are aimed at international (business) guests. A large part of the business guests come from the US. In their wake, museums, amusement parks and restaurants will feel the pain. American guests spend above-average money on entertainment, shopping and the catering industry.

metadata.source_title: Geopolitics under the microscope: impact on retail and leisure

text: We also do not expect strong growth in Chinese tourism. After all, the economic prospects for China have been adjusted downwards slightly due to the export shock that the country is experiencing. [The Economics Department of ABN AMRO](https://www.abnamro.com/research/nl/onze-research/china-the-art-of-the-no-deal2) expects gross domestic product (GDP) growth of 4.1 percent this year. Even before the import duties, the NBTC expected that the number of guests in 2019 would not be matched again until 2033. Chinese tourism in Europe fell sharply during the corona pandemic and growth has remained slow since then. Group travel in particular, which generated the largest volumes, is slowly recovering. The focus is still on short trips within Asia.

metadata.source_title: Geopolitics under the microscope: impact on retail and leisure

text: Conversely, the demand for travel from the Netherlands to the US is also declining due to political unrest, dissatisfaction with Trump's policy and stricter border controls, the [FD](https://fd.nl/bedrijfsleven/1552537/vliegreizen-naar-de-vs-maken-diepvlucht) indicated in mid-April. Short city trips to New York, for example, remain popular, but interest in longer tours is decreasing. American and international airlines and hotel groups are feeling the impact of the reduced demand, but Dutch travel organizations that generate a large part of their turnover from organizing trips to the US may also notice it. Apparently the objections mentioned do not outweigh the lower dollar exchange rate, which actually makes staying in the US cheaper.

metadata.source_title: Geopolitics under the microscope: impact on retail and leisure

text: Despite the decline in tourism outside Europe, catering entrepreneurs could benefit from the lower dollar. Raw materials such as coffee and cocoa are often paid for in dollars, and in theory these products then become relatively cheaper for Dutch catering entrepreneurs. In theory, because although a weak dollar makes raw materials cheaper, the catering entrepreneur does not immediately notice this. There are often three to four links between the international commodity market and a cup of coffee in the café, such as trading houses and wholesalers who often work with long-term contracts. Finally, other costs also play a role in the price of a cup of coffee, such as those for personnel.

metadata.source_title: Geopolitics under the microscope: impact on retail and leisure

text: Indirectly, all the uncertainty in the world can also lead to cautious consumers, despite the buffers they have built up. The purchasing power of many Dutch people has improved considerably in the past year due to wage increases and lower inflation. In addition, households will have saved no less than 24 billion euros in 2024, resulting in a total bank balance of 600 billion euros. On the other hand, consumers remain cautious, which is reflected in the low and even declining consumer confidence in recent months, down to -37 in April. Most likely, geopolitical uncertainties play a role in this.

metadata.source_title: Geopolitics under the microscope: impact on retail and leisure

text: In times of uncertainty, consumers are inclined to postpone unnecessary purchases, opt for private labels and discounters more often and reconsider major expenditure on travel or renovation, for example. Consumers are less likely to make impulse purchases; they prefer to wait a while. In addition, there may be a greater need for security in travel and leisure. People will then book their holidays later, especially in the event of geopolitical unrest. This means that short, more local trips win out over long-distance trips.

metadata.source_title: Geopolitics under the microscope: impact on retail and leisure

text: ## Entrepreneur uncertainty and alternative business strategies

metadata.source_title: Geopolitics under the microscope: impact on retail and leisure

text: All geopolitical uncertainties can be a reason for entrepreneurs to take measures. For example, they can build up extra stock out of fear of even higher import duties or delivery delays. Temporary additional purchases naturally require available storage space, cash flow and some risk acceptance. Some products are also difficult to purchase in advance, such as fashion and fresh produce.

metadata.source_title: Geopolitics under the microscope: impact on retail and leisure

text: Uncertainty surrounding deliveries can also mean that retailers prefer to choose a slightly more expensive but stable supplier instead of the cheapest parties. Retailers can also choose multiple suppliers for the same products. This reduces the negative consequences of an actual supply disruption.

metadata.source_title: Geopolitics under the microscope: impact on retail and leisure

text: As indicated, some of the retailers also have their own production in-house. In response to geopolitical developments, they could focus on 'reshoring' and 'friendshoring', where they locate production closer to home or in 'safe countries'. This results in higher costs, but reduces dependency in the longer term.

metadata.source_title: Geopolitics under the microscope: impact on retail and leisure

text: For the time being, Dutch retailers do not appear to be using other business strategies on a large scale, according to the CBS Business Survey measured at the beginning of March 2025. Almost half of entrepreneurs say they do not adjust their business strategy or do not intend to do so and around a third indicate that they only have domestic activities. Reshoring and friendshoring takes place in 4.3 percent of retailers and 1.5 percent of car dealers. Changing countries from which goods, such as raw materials, are obtained or from countries to which products are sent, affects almost 6 percent of the entrepreneurs surveyed. Finally, with regard to building additional stock, this applies to 4 percent of retailers and more than 2 percent of car dealers.

metadata.source_title: Geopolitics under the microscope: impact on retail and leisure

text: Although the direct impact of geopolitical tensions and trade tariffs on the Dutch retail and leisure sector appears limited for the time being, it is the indirect influence that entrepreneurs should not underestimate. Fluctuating currency exchange rates, risk of dumping, shifting consumer behavior and changing travel intentions require alertness and flexibility. Thinking ahead, distribution of suppliers, scenario analyzes and adjustment of purchasing and pricing strategies can help to limit risks and seize opportunities in an increasingly unpredictable world.

metadata.source_title: Geopolitics under the microscope: impact on retail and leisure

text: Today's store is more than just a pantry. It offers customers advice and inspiration and is the place to be in contact with customers. Due to the Ukraine crisis and significant inflation, consumer confidence has fallen sharply and this affects consumer behavior. Retailers are also faced with significant cost increases in the areas of purchasing, rent, personnel costs, Covid recovery payments and energy. Circumstances remain challenging for many retailers even after Covid.
